era_context: The mid-15th century is a period of significant transition in Europe and Asia. The Hundred Years' War between England and France has recently ended (1453), reshaping Western European politics. The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power after the conquest of Constantinople. The Ming Dynasty in China is stable under centralised rule. The Italian Renaissance is flourishing. In Africa, Portuguese explorers are establishing trading posts along the coast.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Indigenous Americas (no written records in European tradition)

events.title: Death of Cosimo de' Medici (Cosimo the Elder)
events.description: Cosimo de' Medici, known as Cosimo the Elder, died on 1 August 1464 at the age of 86. He had been the de facto ruler of Florence since the 1430s and was a major patron of the arts, learning, and architecture. His death marked the end of an era in Florentine politics and the beginning of the succession of his son Piero de' Medici (Piero the Gouty). Cosimo's legacy was the establishment of the Medici family as the dominant power in Florence and a major patron of the Renaissance.

sources.name: Giorgio Vasari, Lives of the Artists

events.certainty_note: Cosimo de' Medici's death on 1 August 1464 is precisely documented in Florentine records.
cross_references: Part of Medici family dominance in Florence (1434–1494)

events.title: Chenghua Emperor ascends to Ming throne
events.description: Zhu Jianshen, who would become known as the Chenghua Emperor, ascended to the Ming throne in 1464 at the age of 18. He succeeded his father, the Zhengtong Emperor (who had been deposed and then restored). The Chenghua Emperor's reign would be marked by administrative consolidation and the growing influence of eunuchs in court politics. His accession represented a transition in Ming leadership and the continuation of centralised imperial rule.
key_figures: Zhu Qizhen (Zhengtong Emperor, his father, previous ruler)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Chenghua Emperor's accession in 1464 is precisely documented in Ming court records.
cross_references: Part of Ming Dynasty political history (1368–1644)

events.title: Thirteen Years' War between Poland-Lithuania and Teutonic Order continues
events.description: The Thirteen Years' War (1454–1466) between the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth and the Teutonic Order was ongoing in 1464. The conflict had been initiated by the Prussian Confederation's appeal to Poland against Teutonic rule. In 1464, both sides were engaged in military campaigns and diplomatic negotiations. The war would continue for two more years before concluding with the Peace of Thorn in 1466.
key_figures: Ludwig von Vietinghoff (Grand Master of the Teutonic Order)
events.certainty_note: The Thirteen Years' War is well-documented; 1464 falls within the confirmed period of conflict.

events.title: Mehmed II consolidates Ottoman power; campaigns in Anatolia and Balkans
events.description: Mehmed II, the Conqueror, continued his consolidation of Ottoman power in 1464. Following the conquest of Constantinople in 1453, Mehmed was systematically subjugating rival powers in Anatolia and the Balkans. In 1464, he was engaged in military campaigns to expand Ottoman territory and eliminate threats to Ottoman hegemony. His reign marked the transformation of the Ottoman state from a regional power to an empire.
events.certainty_note: Mehmed II's military activities are well-documented in Ottoman and Venetian sources.

events.title: Italian Renaissance: Piero della Francesca and Sandro Botticelli active
events.description: In 1464, major figures of the Italian Renaissance were active and producing significant works. Piero della Francesca was completing major commissions in Urbino and Arezzo, while Sandro Botticelli was establishing himself as a major painter in Florence. The year 1464 was a period of intense artistic activity and innovation in Italy, with the Renaissance in full flourish. These artists were developing new techniques in perspective, light, and form that would define Renaissance art.
key_figures: Federico da Montefeltro (Duke of Urbino, patron)

sources.name: Giorgio Vasari, Lives of the Artists

events.certainty_note: The careers of Piero della Francesca and Sandro Botticelli are well-documented, though specific works and dates can sometimes vary.
cross_references: Part of Italian Renaissance cultural flourishing (14th–16th centuries)

events.title: Portuguese exploration: Prince Henry the Navigator's patronage continues
events.description: Prince Henry the Navigator of Portugal continued his patronage of exploration in 1464. Portuguese explorers were systematically charting the African coast and establishing trading posts. In 1464, Portuguese merchants and explorers were active in Guinea and along the West African coast, establishing the foundations for Portuguese commercial dominance in the region. Henry's patronage was instrumental in launching the Age of Exploration.

sources.name: Portuguese royal archives and navigation records

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration of this period is documented, though specific dates for individual voyages can vary.
cross_references: Part of Portuguese Age of Exploration (1415–1500s)

disconfirming_evidence: No significant disconfirming evidence identified for the events listed. However, the characterisation of Mehmed II's reign as purely military expansion is somewhat simplified; modern scholarship emphasises the administrative and cultural developments of his reign as well. Additionally, the role of Prince Henry the Navigator in Portuguese exploration has been somewhat mythologised; while he was a significant patron, the exploration was driven by multiple factors including commercial interests and royal policy.
historiographic_note: The year 1464 is well-documented in European sources, particularly for political and military events. Italian Renaissance sources are particularly rich. Ming Dynasty records are extensive and contemporary. However, as with other years in this period, non-European regions are underrepresented. The sources available are often written from the perspective of European powers or East Asian courts. The historical record for 1464 is heavily weighted toward European and East Asian events, with limited documentation of events in Africa, the Americas, and Central Asia.

graph_edges.note: Cosimo de' Medici's death led to Piero de' Medici's succession
Chenghua Emperor's accession began his reign
The Thirteen Years' War continued and concluded in 1466
